Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege stands as one of the most sophisticated tactical shooters ever created, blending intense strategy with beautifully crafted environments and deeply rewarding gameplay. Its maps are more than simple battlegrounds; they are intricate spaces filled with verticality, destruction, and carefully designed angles that make every round feel dynamic and unpredictable. The gunplay is sharp and deliberate, rewarding precision, patience, and composure rather than reckless aggression, while the game’s vast array of utility — from drones and breaching tools to traps and surveillance gadgets — creates endless strategic possibilities where information can be just as powerful as firepower. Although Siege possesses a notoriously steep learning curve, that challenge is precisely what makes it so compelling, as every mistake becomes a lesson and every improvement feels genuinely earned. Few multiplayer games capture tension, teamwork, and tactical depth with such elegance, making Siege an experience that remains both intellectually engaging and endlessly exhilarating.

I've been playing this game since 2016-17 and have spent countless hours on it, both alone and with friends. This game isn’t made for everyone — either you adapt and get good at it, or you go back to COD. If you can’t hear the slightest sound, you’re already dead. Rainbow Six Siege isn’t just about having good aim: it’s about map knowledge, recoil control, communication, teamwork, patience, positioning, and understanding how people think and play. One small mistake can cost the entire round.

That’s what makes Siege so frustrating at times, but also one of the most rewarding shooters ever made. I’ve destroyed a monitor, a mouse, and a keyboard after some unbelievably ***** games. Somehow, I even became a furry because of this game — don’t ask me how, because I honestly don’t know either.

With Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege now being free-to-play, it’s obviously hard not to recommend a game where you lose nothing by trying it. However, for anyone trying to get into it for anything more than casual quick play or some chill matches with friends, it can feel like a waste of time. The fact that the game is made by Ubisoft should already tell you enough. 
More often than not, you’ll encounter some kind of bug or glitch that makes the game far more annoying than it already is. Not to mention the server issues that can kick you from matches and cost you rank points.

The lobbies are also quite often filled with toxic people with zero mental stability, but you should already be used to that if you’ve played any competitive shooter before.

To give the game some credit, the guns feel amazing, and it’s genuinely rewarding when you finally learn how to control a high-recoil weapon with an ACOG. Another strong point of Siege is the huge variety of operators it has gained throughout its 10+ year lifespan. While it takes a while for new players to learn all of them, I’m confident most players will eventually find a couple of operators on both attack and defense that suit their playstyle really well.

To sum it up, I wouldn’t recommend Siege for highly competitive players. But hey, if you’re just looking for a random game to mess around in with the boys, then go right ahead and give it a try, but with the amount of problems the game has, I still have to put this up as a negative review.

Classic Ubisoft problem of having a genius idea for a game, something that would be easy money for almost any other developer, and steadily making it worse and worse to the point where the game is so fundamentally unrewarding that the community will inevitably become toxic. 

The balancing is horrific, with some characters being more oriented towards gunplay and kills while others are gadget oriented, and a final third class of operators with both. Characters like Aruni and Deimos simply should not exist when you have others like Glaz and Jaeger who, in comparison, are terrible. The game also launches incorrectly about half the time, which is likely a result of ubisoft connect being a useless and obsolete service. Matchmaking is another horrific choice, as it seems that every match comes down to a competition between one or two people with 12 kills and a virtually useless team that struggles to reach a positive kd. You will inevitably fall on either side of this equation, with neither being fun or at all rewarding. The "hidden mmr" system seems like a terrible idea, as you will reach the skill level of a higher tier yet be stuck playing against others who have only picked up the game again midway through the season or players two or sometimes three ranks above you. The only reason this game is still played is its unique concept, as the combination of hero shooter with (semi) realistic fps is something you can't really find elsewhere. This does not prevent Ubisoft from trying, to the best of their ability, to introduce nonsensical, unbalanced, stupid characters with the most awful writing you will find outside of a fanfiction page. With the addition of Snake, there are now four (five if you count Grim) characters whose abilities center around being able to track you around the map, through walls and whatever else there might be. You might wonder: How does Ubisoft balance something like that? They don't. Deimos has two of the best primaries in the game, however when he tracks he then has to take out his secondary revolver with full penetration and a damage score higher than Glaz, who was the original sniper character in the game. This secondary weapon both does more damage and has significantly less recoil than Glaz's primary, as while Deimos must be firing a high caliber, he apparently has the super strength to rapid fire a revolver with no movement. Of course, this is just one example of the horribly designed operators in the game, of which there are a few who utterly decimate the rest of the roster. 

I cannot recommend against this game enough, as while it is fun, it incentivizes playstyles that are against the core concept of the game. Would anyone under "siege" simply step outside of the fortified building to shoot at their attackers? No, that's stupid. Yet it is undoubtedly the best (and depending on the map, the only) way to win defense. The repercussion for doing so is a timer which at the end will reveal that you are outside. So what? The attackers have probably heard the barricade break either way, and even if they weren't aware, the icon showing the defender blends in fairly well with those of teammates under quick review. With the number of offensive trackers in the game, the attacker's parallel to this is to simply shoot defenders through walls, with the only indication that they even know you're there often being simply a message that 'whoever' has just pinged you. Often no counter, no response other than to sprint away, which then reveals your location again via the noise. 

Siege has the innate ability to be fun, but just like For Honor, and Assassin's Creed, and Ghost Recon, and anything else Ubisoft touches, they will inevitably ruin the experience through terrible writing and implementations that are tone-deaf and unbelievably stupid. For example, shield operators, when paired together, are overpowered and outright impossible for some operators to counter. What is the response? Not an in-depth reworking to shields, but instead a future operator whose gadget will target shields. Yippee. In half a season, a single ban will make them worthless and teams can shield rush again. What was the response to this idea? Applause from a bunch of idiots. The gameplay loop of siege is so irritating that it fosters what can only be described as outright hatred between players. You will often see your own teammates harass you throughout the match for anything from failing to clutch a 1v3 to reinforcing the wrong wall. You will be blamed for throwing the game despite being first or second on the team. This is what happens when normal people hoping to unwind are met with constant losses to pixel peaks and obvious scripting. 

I would like to reiterate that the writing is both bad and unnecessary, and whoever thought that the swat team game needed romance between characters should have been fired whenever they first opened their mouth. I would mute operator voicelines, but I then run into the problem of people not screaming when shot or on fire, because I guess the dialogue is just that important. I can't wait to see if Frost and Fenrir get married, I hope they add their heckin sweet babirino as an operator to the game.

Rainbow Six Siege is not installed on my computer. It has infested it.

I don’t launch the game anymore. It summons me. I’ll be doing something normal; eating, sleeping, experiencing brief moments of peace, and suddenly I feel it. A pull. A whisper. “Just one match.” It’s never one match. It’s never been one match. Time does not function inside Siege. I have aged.

The operators are not real people. They are archetypes of suffering. Every Ash main moves like they’ve seen the code of the universe and rejected it. Every Doc on my team develops a rare condition where they physically cannot heal anyone except themselves, usually after the round has already been lost. And there is always one player who moves with the confidence of a prophet and the awareness of a Roomba.

I load into a match and immediately feel like I’ve entered a legally questionable experiment where four strangers are assigned to test how quickly I lose trust in humanity. By round two, I no longer recognize myself. By round three, I’m apologizing to inanimate objects.

Gunfights are less about skill and more about who the universe decides to favor in that exact millisecond. I’ve been eliminated by people I never saw, never heard, and I’m not entirely convinced were real. Just a killcam of a man emerging from drywall like he pays rent there.

My teammates communicate exclusively through sighs, silence, or acts of betrayal. The only time they use their microphones is to say something that fundamentally alters my understanding of human intelligence. Meanwhile, the enemy team moves like a synchronized organism. I am not fighting five people. I am fighting a single, hateful entity that has decided I need to be humbled.

At some point, you stop trying to win. You start trying to understand. Why did that happen? Why am I here? Why did my teammate reinforce me into a room like I’m being contained?

And then, against all logic, you clutch. A perfect, impossible moment. A clean entry. A flawless angle. Everything aligns. You feel powerful. Enlightened. Chosen.  Time slows down. You win the round and finally achieve inner peace.

The very next round you are immediately removed from existence by a man who was never on your screen.

I don’t think this is a game. I think this is a long-form psychological study and I am both the subject and the control group.

9/10. I feel different as a person.

I'm sure you can already tell by how excited I am to share my opinion on playing R6. You know, there is this one saying in life that goes: "It can't get any worse, or can it?" - As for somebody that never played R6, this phrase perfectly epitomises my entire experience. I'll get straight to the point.

How incompetent as gaming dev, do you have to be, to put a lvl 30 unranked, in a champions lobby, playing freaking quick match? I am only 50 hours into R6 and I've yet to play ranked, because I am softlocked by lvl 1000 ranked anxiety riddled parasocial degenerates, who refuse to play different gamemodes, literally preventing me from winning games and hitting lvl 50.

Holy bonkers, is he above me? Under me? Or inside of me? Where is what coming from? The audio in this game greatly enables my schizophrenia. I feel like a clueless buffoon most of the time, trying to pre-fire angles, to pretend like I know what I'm doing, when I'm clearly lost.

Callouts? What are those? Ah, my fault! "He's at that window!", "The one in the corridor!", "The one on third floor!", "The one facing the piano!" Is this why no one ever talks? Including me? Compare this to CS2 callouts: "Yo, they're coming B!", "One is mid!", "Two out A!" Hear the difference?

Blitz shield, Ying clusters, Ela mines, Zofia KS7, Echo drone, Fenrir Dreadmine, Twitch Drone. Yeah, if I'm not wrong, there are approximately 10 ways, if not more, to blind/stun your opponents. Gotta make sure I don't get a seizure at this point. After loading up R6 a couple of times now, I realised why they give you an epilepsy warning at the start of the game.

I may or may not have picked the absolute worst time to try out R6. The knowledge gap between a beginner and a "Shaiiko wannabe" is as wide as the distance between earth and proxima centauri b. Cannot imagine a newbie without any fps experience downloading Rainbow 6 Siege today, playing till lvl 50 and going: "Yeah, that went well."

I'm close to being immune to toxicity, but having my teammate blow my brains out after having accidentally destroyed his drone by a friction of my frag grenade that was used to break down the front entrance of a building, made me think of atleast 11 possible slurs. Not saying I used any, since I do respect the reputation system, which does benefit me with some of that GOLD GOLD GOLD action.

I did enjoy playing the 1v1 gamemode, since there is no one to blame but yourself. Makes you have less of an ego, when you do get outclassed. Hope valve steals that idea and puts it into cs2. Also recently found out that getting killed through walls in this game is a really common occurrence. 

To this day, I feel like most of the "veterans" themselves cannot even comprehend some of the things that do happen in R6. Usually, watching esports helps me understand new video games a lot better. There is a major coming up, in Salt Lake City. Can't wait for that. It will probably be my first and last tournament I watch from R6.

Rainbow Six Siege is basically Pokémon.

When it started, it was simple. You had like eight monster types. You counter a reinforced wall with the guy carrying thermite. You counter bullets with the guy holding a big shield. Easy.

Now? Now we are in the era of OVERLORD MAX DARK STEEL POISON TYPE. You are sitting there screaming because someone just perfectly countered your FLYING DIABETIC WATER BUG TYPE setup... and you have absolutely no idea how they knew.

It is literally a game with homework attached. You can't just log in and shoot; you have to study….

But here is the absolute worst part. When you forget to do that homework... it isn't the teacher who gets mad at you. It is the entire class. And they do not just get a little annoyed. The community reacts like your one missed assignment means the whole class now has to do wind sprints... uphill... in the pouring rain... until you either magically finish the homework right then and there or just leave the school because why would you even be here disgracing us without doing the homework.

In example: You put up a barricade in the wrong doorway or pick an operator that doesn't perfectly fit the current meta... and suddenly four strangers are screaming in your headset like you just committed an international war crime. They start giving testimony to the lobby like they are on trial at the Hague; building a legal case for why you specifically deserve to go to federal prison for picking the wrong character.

There is no casual mode anymore. You either log in with a PhD in Siege... or you are the kid making everyone else run laps.

I’ve never played LoL so my opinion on toxic communities could be one-upped…but we will save that for the debate of “whats worse: a flat tire on the freeway or strep throat?”

- Just a Paul Review

This game is basically what happens if a SWAT simulator, a chess match, and a toxic group chat had a baby… and then gave that baby a gun and anxiety.

At its core, Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege is NOT a shooter—it’s a stress simulator where drywall is your worst enemy. You don’t walk through doors, you question their entire existence. Walls? Optional. Floors? Temporary. Your sanity? Gone by round 3.

🧠 Gameplay (aka “overthinking: the game”)

You don’t just aim and shoot—you:

drone like you’re in the FBI
argue with your team about which wall to reinforce
die instantly because someone made a tiny hole in a wall 2 minutes ago

It rewards big brain plays over raw aim, which is cool… until you get one-tapped by a dude named “xX_DoorCamper_Xx” who hasn’t moved since 2017. The game is praised for this tactical depth and teamwork focus, but it can also feel brutally punishing

📚 Learning curve (aka “welcome to hell”)

New players:

don’t know the maps
don’t know operators
don’t know why they died

Veterans:

still don’t know what just happened

Even Reddit sums it up perfectly:

“The game sucks but you’ll love it”

And honestly? That’s the most accurate review ever written.

💸 Monetization (aka “Ubisoft wants your lunch money”)
You bought the game? Cool.
Now grind 25 hours for one operator… or pay up.

People have been side-eyeing the grind and microtransactions for YEARS, with complaints about slow progression and constant monetization pressure

☠️ Community (aka “psychological warfare”)

You will experience:

teammates yelling slurs over voice chat
getting team-killed because you picked the “wrong” operator
ranked games that feel like hostage negotiations

Also… cheating and toxicity? Yeah, still a thing, and players regularly complain about it, especially at higher ranks

🔥 The good (because somehow there is some)
When it clicks? It’s one of the most intense FPS experiences ever
Winning with a coordinated team feels like you just completed a heist
Every round feels like life or death because… it kinda is (no respawns)
🧾 Final verdict

Rainbow Six Siege is:

40% tactical genius
30% anxiety
20% getting yelled at
10% wondering why you still play

It’s the kind of game you uninstall… then reinstall… then complain about… then queue again.

Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege
Rating: Recommended

If you’re looking for a relaxing tactical shooter to play after work while you listen to a podcast, keep walking. Rainbow Six Siege isn't just a game; it’s a 15-minute panic attack that you voluntarily sign up for. It is the only game where the sound of a single Velcro strap ripping or a faint "thud" through a ceiling can make your heart rate jump to 120 BPM.

The Experience
The magic (and the misery) of Siege lies in the silence. * The Setup: You’ve spent the prep phase reinforcing walls, hiding gadgets, and checking cams. Then, the round starts, and for 45 seconds, there is nothing but the ambient hum of the map.

The Tension: You’re holding an angle through a hole the size of a coin. You know they're coming. You hear a floorboard creak. Your palms are sweating. You’re holding your breath in real life because you’re convinced the enemy can hear you breathe through your headset.

The Chaos: One explosion. That’s all it takes. The wall behind you vanishes, flashbangs turn your screen white, and suddenly you’re in a 1v3 situation with 20 seconds on the clock.

The 1vX Clutch
There is no "high" in gaming quite like being the last person alive on your team. Every teammate is watching your POV. The pressure is suffocating. When you finally pull off that flick shot or outsmart the last attacker with a well-placed C4, the adrenaline dump is so intense you’ll need to stare at a wall for five minutes after the match just to recalibrate.

The Verdict
Siege is a toxic, beautiful, frustrating, and brilliant mess. It has a learning curve that looks like a vertical cliff face, and the community will judge your every move. But for those moments where your heart is pounding against your ribs and you manage to survive by the skin of your teeth?

There is absolutely nothing else like it.

Pros:

Deepest tactical gameplay on the market.

Destruction physics that actually matter.

Unmatched tension.

Cons:

Might actually shorten your lifespan due to stress.

The learning curve is brutal.

You will hear "Cope" or "L" at least once an hour.

Ultimate crème brûlée
Ingredients:
2 cartons double cream
1 large (284ml) plus 1 small (142ml)
100ml whole milk
1 vanilla pod
5 egg yolks
50g golden caster sugar
plus extra for the topping

step 1
Heat the oven to 180C/160C fan/gas 4. Sit four 175ml ramekins in a deep roasting tin at least 7.5cm deep (or a large deep cake tin), one that will enable a baking tray to sit well above the ramekins when laid across the top of the tin.

step 2
Pour the large and small cartons of double cream into a medium pan with the milk.

step 3
Lay the vanilla pod on a board and slice lengthways through the middle with a sharp knife to split it in two. Use the tip of the knife to scrape out all the tiny seeds into the cream mixture. Drop the vanilla pod in as well, and set aside.

step 4
Put the egg yolks and sugar in a mixing bowl and whisk for 1 min with an electric hand whisk until paler in colour and a bit fluffy.

step 5
Put the pan with the cream on a medium heat and bring almost to the boil. As soon as you see bubbles appear round the edge, take the pan off the heat.

step 6
Pour the hot cream into the beaten egg yolks, stirring with a wire whisk as you do so, and scraping out the seeds from the pan.

step 7
Set a fine sieve over a large wide jug or bowl and pour the hot mixture through to strain it, encouraging any stray vanilla seeds through at the end.

step 8
Using a big spoon, scoop off all the pale foam that is sitting on the top of the liquid (this will be several spoonfuls) and discard. Give the mixture a stir.

step 9
Pour in enough hot water (from the tap) into the roasting tin to come about 1.5cm up the sides of the ramekins. Pour the hot cream into the ramekins so you fill them up right to the top – it’s easier to spoon in the last little bit.

step 10
Put them in the oven and lay a baking sheet over the top of the tin so it sits well above the ramekins and completely covers them, but not the whole tin, leaving a small gap at one side to allow air to circulate.

step 11
Bake for 30-35 mins until the mixture is softly set. To check, gently sway the roasting tin and if the crème brûlées are ready, they will wobble a bit like a jelly in the middle. Don’t let them get too firm.

step 12
Lift the ramekins out of the roasting tin with oven gloves and set them on a wire rack to cool for a couple of minutes only, then put in the fridge to cool completely. This can be done overnight without affecting the texture.

step 13
When ready to serve, wipe round the top edge of the dishes, sprinkle 1½ tsp of caster sugar over each ramekin and spread it out with the back of a spoon to completely cover.

step 14
Spray with a little water using a fine spray (the sort you buy in a craft shop) to just dampen the sugar – then use a blow torch to caramelise it. Hold the flame just above the sugar and keep moving it round and round until caramelised. Serve when the brûlée is firm, or within an hour or two.

That being said, Mooda cheated on his older account, got banned due to signing in into his old account and Ubisoft easily detected the same PC via same login and banned his new account. L Mooda, tho I believe he stopped cheating after he got caught long ago on his old account.

Now about the game:

It's entirely an AIM duel, and allot of it has to do with Map knowledge and the type of agent you play & your opponent/s play.

In most basic terms, If you have really good aim and map knowledge, you may get to Champion.
If you have really good aim, map knowledge, and know all your defender/attack agents and how they work and how to play agains't them or how to play with them, you may just go Pro if you care enough.

Other than that, The game feels fun to play, I haven't played the game enough yet to know just how many cheaters there are, tho I do see smurfs. And also the game has it's problems with False Bans, which kinda sucks, since many streamers/pros that played the game got falsely banned and then the ban get's revoked. Should that be a thing in any game? No. So that sucks.

If you wanna try out the game, expect a hard learning curve, you will die allot, you might hit your table for losing to something so stupid (a trap, knife) etc; But eventually if you play enough and learn the maps, aim better, learn the agents, you will get better and you will have "fun".

This game is broken, just like it made my mental health. I used to play a bunch on console, so I've spent well over 1,000 hours in this game. Constant bugs; rampant cheats; developers that care more about money than player base. This game had ALL the recipes for failure, and yet it's still alive. That's because from a gameplay standpoint, it really is one of a kind. When you start playing and learn a little bit about the game, it feels magical and it feels very good to hit your peak rank and keep climbing; until you realize that ranked 2.0 is a thing and your rank means but naught.
Back to the mental health thing, this game is SO bad for you. It's more addictive than heroin, and I would argue it's much more harmful. I've had plenty of fights with friends over a damn VIDEO GAME, and it makes me mad enough to rub off on the people around me. Playing is fun, and still somehow feels like torture.
Anyways I digress, and I'm sad to say it, but after 4 years, I officially quit Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege :(.
